is a city in [Morgan County](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Georgia "Morgan County, Georgia"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)"), United States. It is part of the [Atlanta-Athens-Clarke-Sandy Springs combined statistical area](/wiki/Atlanta_metropolitan_area "Atlanta metropolitan area"). The population was 4,447 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) up from 3,979 in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"). The city is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Morgan County](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Georgia "Morgan County, Georgia") and the site of the [Morgan County Courthouse](/wiki/Morgan_County_Courthouse_(Georgia) "Morgan County Courthouse (Georgia)").

**Madison** is a city in [Morgan County](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Georgia "Morgan County, Georgia"), [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(U.S._state) "Georgia (U.S. state)"), United States. It is part of the [Atlanta-Athens-Clarke-Sandy Springs combined statistical area](/wiki/Atlanta_metropolitan_area "Atlanta metropolitan area"). The population was 4,447 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[3]](#cite_note-Census_2020-3) up from 3,979 in [2010](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"). The city is the [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of [Morgan County](/wiki/Morgan_County,_Georgia "Morgan County, Georgia") and the site of the [Morgan County Courthouse](/wiki/Morgan_County_Courthouse_(Georgia) "Morgan County Courthouse (Georgia)").

The [Madison Historic District](/wiki/Madison_Historic_District_(Madison,_Georgia) "Madison Historic District (Madison, Georgia)") is one of the largest in the state.[[6]](#cite_note-TheHistoricalNews-6) Many of the nearly 100 antebellum homes have been carefully restored. [Bonar Hall](/wiki/Bonar_Hall "Bonar Hall") is one of the first of the grand-style [Federal](/wiki/Federal_architecture "Federal architecture") homes built in Madison during the town's [cotton-boom](/wiki/King_Cotton "King Cotton") heyday from 1840 to 1860.

*Budget Travel* magazine voted Madison as one of the world's 16 most picturesque villages.[[7]](#cite_note-budgettravel-7)

Madison is featured on Georgia's Antebellum Trail, and is designated as one of the state's Historic Heartland cities.

### Early 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Early 19th century")]

On December 12, 1809, the town, [named for](/wiki/Namesake "Namesake") [4th](/wiki/List_of_Presidents_of_the_United_States "List of Presidents of the United States") [United States president](/wiki/President_of_the_United_States "President of the United States"), [James Madison](/wiki/James_Madison "James Madison"), was [incorporated](/wiki/Municipal_corporation "Municipal corporation").[[8]](#cite_note-8) Madison was described in an early 19th-century issue of *White's Statistics of Georgia* as "the most cultured and aristocratic town on the stagecoach route from [Charleston](/wiki/Charleston,_South_Carolina "Charleston, South Carolina") to [New Orleans](/wiki/New_Orleans "New Orleans")."[[6]](#cite_note-TheHistoricalNews-6) An 1849 edition of *White's Statistics* stated, "In point of intelligence, refinement, and hospitality, this town acknowledges no superior."[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/43/CarterHousephoto.JPG/250px-CarterHousephoto.JPG)](/wiki/File:CarterHousephoto.JPG)

[Historic District of Madison](/wiki/Madison_Historic_District_(Madison,_Georgia) "Madison Historic District (Madison, Georgia)"), 2010

While many believe that [William Tecumseh Sherman](/wiki/William_Tecumseh_Sherman "William Tecumseh Sherman") spared the town because it was too beautiful to burn during his [March to the Sea](/wiki/Sherman%27s_March_to_the_Sea "Sherman's March to the Sea"), the truth is that Madison was home to pro-Union Congressman (later Senator) [Joshua Hill](/wiki/Joshua_Hill_(politician) "Joshua Hill (politician)"). Hill had ties with General Sherman's brother in the House of Representatives, so his sparing the town was more political than appreciation of its beauty.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Jim Crow era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Jim Crow era")]

In 1895 Madison was reported to have an oil mill with a capital of $35,000, a soap factory, a fertilizer factory, four steam [ginneries](/wiki/Cotton_gin "Cotton gin"), a mammoth compress, two [carriage](/wiki/Carriage "Carriage") factories, a furniture factory, a grist and flouringmill, a bottling works, a distillery with a capacity of 120 gallons a day, an ice factory with a capital of $10,500, a canning factory with a capital of $10,000, a bank with a capital of $75,000, surplus $12,000, and a number of small industries operated by individual enterprise.[[10]](#cite_note-afruitparadiseis00cott-10) One of the carriage factories was owned and operated by prominent African-American businessman and entrepreneur H. R. Goldwire.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Against the backdrop of this [Jim Crow-era](/wiki/Jim_Crow_laws "Jim Crow laws") prosperity, white Madisonians participated in at least three documented [lynchings](/wiki/Lynching "Lynching") of African Americans. In February 1890, after a rushed trial involving knife-wielding [jurors](/wiki/Jurors "Jurors"), Brown Washington, a 15-year-old,[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11) was found guilty of the murder of a 9-year-old local white girl. After the verdict, though the sheriff with the governor's approval called up the Madison Home Guard to protect Washington, "only three militiamen and none of the officers" responded to the order. Washington was thus easily taken from jail by a posse of ten men organized by a "leading local businessman".[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) Described as "among the best citizens", they promptly handed him over to a mob of over 300 people waiting outside the courthouse. From there, he was taken to a [telegraph pole](/wiki/Telegraph_pole "Telegraph pole") behind a local residence, allowed a prayer, then strung up and shot, his body mutilated by more than 100 bullets. Afterwards, in the patriarchal exhibition-style common of southern lynchings, a sign was posted on the telegraph pole: "Our women and children will be protected."[[11]](#cite_note-:3-11)[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12) His body was not taken down until noon the next day.[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)

According to Brundage's account of the lynching of Brown Washington in *Lynching in the New South: Georgia and Virginia, 1880-1930*:

> The open participation of men 'of all ages and standing in life,' the carefully organized public meeting that planned the mob's course of action, the obvious complicity of the militia, and the ritualized execution of Washington all highlight the degree to which the lynching was sanctioned by the community at large. Shared attitudes toward women, sexuality, and black criminality, combined with local bonds of community and family, focused the fears and rage of whites on Washington and guaranteed mass involvement in his execution.[[12]](#cite_note-:0-12)

In the aftermath, though local and state authorities vowed to thoroughly investigate the lynching as well as the Madison Home Guard's dereliction of duty, just a week later a [grand jury](/wiki/Grand_jury "Grand jury") was advised by a judge of the superior court of Madison that any investigation would be a waste of time. In addition, the state body charged with investigating the home guard's non-response reported that their absence had been satisfactorily explained and no tribunal would be convened to investigate the matter."[[13]](#cite_note-:1-13)[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14)

Although the local Madisonian newspaper failed to report on the 1890 extra-judicial murder of Mr. Washington, an even earlier first lynching by Madisonians of a man they similarly pulled out of the old stone county jail appears in the contemporary accounts from the *[Atlanta Constitution](/wiki/Atlanta_Constitution "Atlanta Constitution")*.[[14]](#cite_note-:2-14)

In 1919, ten years after the erection of a [Confederate memorial](/wiki/List_of_Confederate_monuments_and_memorials_in_Georgia "List of Confederate monuments and memorials in Georgia") one block from the newly built Morgan County courthouse, another lynching occurred in the dark of night a few days before [Thanksgiving](/wiki/Thanksgiving_(United_States) "Thanksgiving (United States)"). This time, citizens skipped the show-trials altogether, opting to travel to the home of Mr. Wallace Baynes in what one paper of the day called an "arresting party", though no charges against Mr. Baynes were stipulated in the news account.[[15]](#cite_note-:4-15) Baynes shot at the party, striking Mr. Frank F. Ozburn of Madison in the head, killing him instantly. In response, the mob outside his home grew to 40-50 men. Despite the arrival of Madison Sheriff C.S. Baldwin, Mr. Baynes was pulled from his home by a rope and shot near the Little River. Afterwards, the sheriff present at the lynching said he could not identify any of the men who came for Mr. Baynes, despite the fact that they arrived in cars and lit up Mr. Baynes' home with the headlights of their vehicles.[[15]](#cite_note-:4-15) In an editorial that argued that mobs in the [South](/wiki/Southern_United_States "Southern United States") were no worse than mobs in the [North](/wiki/Northern_United_States "Northern United States") yet condemned future lynchings, the local Madisonian claimed: "There is not now and perhaps will never be, any friction between the races here."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The Confederate monument erected in 1909 by the Morgan County Daughters of the Confederacy one block from the courthouse where Mr. Baynes was not afforded a trial was inscribed in part: "NO NATION ROSE/SO WHITE AND FAIR, NONE FELL SO PURE OF CRIME."[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18) In the 1950s, the monument was moved to Hill Park, a Madison city property donated by Bell Hill Knight, daughter of Joshua Hill, the aforementioned pro-Union senator who before the [Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War") resigned his position rather than support secession. Mrs. Knight, whose husband Captain Gazaway Knight was Commander of the Panola Guards, a Confederate brigade that was organized in Madison, was a staunch member of the Morgan County Daughters of the Confederacy.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Present day

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Present day")]

Madison has one of the largest historic districts in the state of Georgia, with visitors coming to see the [antebellum architecture](/wiki/Antebellum_architecture "Antebellum architecture") of the homes. [Allie Carroll Hart](/wiki/Allie_Carroll_Hart "Allie Carroll Hart") was instrumental in establishing Madison's historical prestige.[[19]](#cite_note-ajc-obit-2003-19)

According to the Madison Historic Preservation Commission, "The Madison Historic District is listed in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") and is Madison's foremost tourist attraction. Preservation of the district and of each property within its boundary provides for the protection of Madison's unique historic character and quality environment. Madison's preservation efforts reflect a nationwide movement to preserve a 'sense of place' amid generic modern development." The Historic Preservation Commission, appointed by Mayor and Council, is charged with protecting the historic character of the district through review of proposed exterior changes.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Geography")]

Madison is located in central Morgan County at [33°35′17″N 83°28′21″W﻿ / ﻿33.58806°N 83.47250°W﻿ / 33.58806; -83.47250](https://geohack.toolforge.org/geohack.php?pagename=Madison,_Georgia&params=33_35_17_N_83_28_21_W_type:city) (33.588038, -83.472368).[[21]](#cite_note-GR1-21) According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 8.9 square miles (23 km2), of which 0.07 square miles (0.18 km2), or 0.82%, are water.[[2]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2022-2)

Madison is situated at an elevation of 691 feet (211 m) on a ridge which traverses Morgan County from the northeast to the southwest.[[22]](#cite_note-22) In Madison, the south side of the ridge drains to tributaries of Sugar Creek, which flows southeast to the [Oconee River](/wiki/Oconee_River "Oconee River"), while the north side drains via Mill Branch to Hard Labor Creek, an east-flowing tributary of the [Apalachee River](/wiki/Apalachee_River_(Georgia) "Apalachee River (Georgia)"), which continues to the Oconee. The southwest part of the city drains to Little Indian Creek, a tributary of the [Little River](/wiki/Little_River_(Oconee_River_tributary) "Little River (Oconee River tributary)"), which flows to the Oconee north of [Milledgeville](/wiki/Milledgeville,_Georgia "Milledgeville, Georgia").

[Interstate 20](/wiki/Interstate_20_in_Georgia "Interstate 20 in Georgia"), [U.S. Route 129](/wiki/U.S._Route_129_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 129 in Georgia"), [U.S. Route 441](/wiki/U.S._Route_441_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 441 in Georgia"), and [U.S. Route 278](/wiki/U.S._Route_278_in_Georgia "U.S. Route 278 in Georgia") pass through Madison. I-20 serves the city from exits 113 and 114, leading east 90 miles (140 km) to [Augusta](/wiki/Augusta,_Georgia "Augusta, Georgia") and west 57 miles (92 km) to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta "Atlanta"). U.S. 278 runs through the center of the city, leading east 19 miles (31 km) to [Greensboro](/wiki/Greensboro,_Georgia "Greensboro, Georgia") and west 24 miles (39 km) to [Covington](/wiki/Covington,_Georgia "Covington, Georgia"). U.S. 129/441 run through the city together, leading north 29 miles (47 km) to [Athens](/wiki/Athens,_Georgia "Athens, Georgia") and south 22 miles (35 km) to [Eatonton](/wiki/Eatonton,_Georgia "Eatonton, Georgia").

## Culture and parks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Culture and parks")]

Madison is home to a handful of art galleries and museums. The [Madison-Morgan Cultural Center](/w/index.php?title=Madison-Morgan_Cultural_Center&action=edit&redlink=1 "Madison-Morgan Cultural Center (page does not exist)") (MMCC) provides a regional focus for performing and visual arts, plus permanent exhibits including a historical exhibit of Georgia's [Piedmont](/wiki/Piedmont_(United_States) "Piedmont (United States)") region. The center occupies an elegantly restored 1895 [Romanesque Revival](/wiki/Romanesque_Revival "Romanesque Revival") building and is located in the heart of Madison's nationally registered historic district. Athens band [R.E.M.](/wiki/R.E.M. "R.E.M.") recorded an *[MTV Unplugged](/wiki/MTV_Unplugged "MTV Unplugged")* session at the center in 1991, where they played "[Losing My Religion](/wiki/Losing_My_Religion "Losing My Religion")" with the [Atlanta Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Atlanta_Symphony_Orchestra "Atlanta Symphony Orchestra").[[25]](#cite_note-remhq-25)

The Morgan County African American Museum is located in Madison.

Heritage Hall is maintained by the Morgan County Historical Society and has been restored for its architectural and historical significance. The original portion of Heritage Hall was built in 1811, and it received its [Greek Revival](/wiki/Greek_Revival "Greek Revival") façade around 1830. The house was a private residence until 1977.

The Madison Artists' Guild has more than 150 members and is a nonprofit organization dedicated to education and the encouragement of artistic endeavors in its members and its community through planned programs and regular gatherings.

There are five parks in the city limits. Wellington, Gilbert, Lambert, and Hill Park are designated for active play, whereas Town Park is designed for events and public gatherings.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Madison's [safari park](/wiki/Safari_park "Safari park"), **Georgia Safari Conservation Park**, opened to the public on June 1, 2024.[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

## Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Crime")]

According to a 2017 crime report produced by the city's planning and development director, property crime rates in Madison are double and triple of nearby [Social Circle](/wiki/Social_Circle,_Georgia "Social Circle, Georgia") and [Watkinsville](/wiki/Watkinsville,_Georgia "Watkinsville, Georgia"), respectively. Violent crime remained steady at a rate of 10 incidents out of a population of 4,034, a rate comparable with Social Circle and Watkinsville. In addition, property crime had decreased in 2016 to a six-year low.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The online analytical platform Niche rates Madison's crime a "C" based on violent and property crime rates.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Madison,_Georgia&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Education")]

The [Morgan County School District](/wiki/Morgan_County_School_District "Morgan County School District") is a [charter school](/wiki/Charter_school "Charter school") system that covers pre-school to grade twelve, and consists of a primary school, an elementary school, a middle school, and a high school.[[31]](#cite_note-31)
In 2023 the district has 230 full-time teachers and over 3,200 students.[[32]](#cite_note-publicschoolreview.com-32) The High School graduation rate is 92%, which is greater than the Georgia average of 85%.[[32]](#cite_note-publicschoolreview.com-32) Overall rankings for the Morgan County School District versus other school districts in Georgia include:

* Highest overall rank (Top 20%)
* Highest math proficiency (Top 10%)
* Highest reading/language arts proficiency (Top 20%)

More detailed statistics for individual schools include:

* **Morgan County Elementary School** Students have an average math proficiency score of 50% (versus the Georgia public elementary school average of 37%), and reading proficiency score of 46% (versus the 40% statewide average).[[32]](#cite_note-publicschoolreview.com-32)
* **Morgan County Middle School** Students have an average math proficiency score of 44% (versus the Georgia public middle school average of 33%), and reading proficiency score of 49% (versus the 41% statewide average).[[32]](#cite_note-publicschoolreview.com-32)
* **Morgan County High School** 39% students are proficient in math (versus the Georgia public high school average of 24%), and reading proficiency matches the 32% statewide average.[[32]](#cite_note-publicschoolreview.com-32)
